name: SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria
description: 10 vendor security controls mapped to the AICPA SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria (2017, with 2022 points of focus), focused on the Security (Common Criteria) and Availability categories. A lighter-weight assessment than the NIST framework, suited to SaaS vendors that publish SOC 2 reports.

controls.title: Security governance and policies established
controls.description: The vendor has a formal information security program: documented security policies, a person or team accountable for security, and management commitment to integrity and competence.
controls.search_query: information security policy security program CISO security team governance security organization management commitment
controls.what_to_look_for: Evidence of a formal security program. Look for: information security policy, security policies reviewed annually, CISO, security officer, security team, security steering committee, code of conduct, security training program, policy acknowledgment, security roles and responsibilities.
controls.what_good_looks_like: CC1.1 requires demonstrated commitment to integrity and ethical values. CC1.4 requires commitment to attract, develop, and retain competent individuals. Best practice: a documented and annually reviewed security policy suite, a named accountable security leader, and mandatory security awareness training for all staff.

scoring_guide.pass: Documented security program with named ownership and policy review cadence
scoring_guide.partial: Security policies mentioned but ownership or review cadence unclear
scoring_guide.fail: Evidence that no formal security program or policies exist
scoring_guide.no_evidence: No mention of security governance, policies, or ownership

controls.title: Risk assessment process in place
controls.description: The vendor periodically identifies and analyzes risks to its objectives — including fraud and technology risks — and updates its risk assessment when the business or threat landscape changes.
controls.search_query: risk assessment risk management process risk register annual risk analysis threat assessment enterprise risk
controls.what_to_look_for: Evidence of a recurring risk assessment practice. Look for: risk assessment, risk register, risk analysis, annual risk review, risk treatment, risk acceptance, threat modeling, risk management framework, likelihood and impact ratings, risk owners.
controls.what_good_looks_like: CC3.2 requires identifying and analyzing risks to the achievement of objectives. CC3.4 requires assessing changes that could significantly impact internal control. Best practice: at least annual formal risk assessments with a maintained risk register, assigned risk owners, and documented treatment decisions.

scoring_guide.pass: Recurring risk assessment described with register, owners, or treatment process
scoring_guide.partial: Risk assessment mentioned but frequency or follow-through unclear
scoring_guide.fail: Evidence that risks are not formally assessed
scoring_guide.no_evidence: No mention of risk assessment or risk management

controls.title: Logical access restricted and authenticated
controls.description: Access to systems and data is restricted to authorized users, protected by strong authentication — including multi-factor authentication — and granted on a least-privilege basis.
controls.search_query: multi-factor authentication MFA logical access controls least privilege role-based access authentication
controls.what_to_look_for: Evidence of restricted, authenticated access. Look for: MFA, 2FA, single sign-on, SSO, role-based access control, RBAC, least privilege, access authorization, unique user IDs, password policy, credential management.
controls.what_good_looks_like: CC6.1 requires logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures to be implemented over protected information assets. Best practice: MFA enforced for all users, unique accounts (no sharing), role-based least-privilege access, and documented authorization workflows.

scoring_guide.pass: MFA and least-privilege access clearly required for all users
scoring_guide.partial: Access controls described but MFA optional, admin-only, or scope unclear
scoring_guide.fail: Evidence of shared accounts or password-only authentication
scoring_guide.no_evidence: No mention of authentication or logical access controls

controls.title: User provisioning and deprovisioning managed
controls.description: New user access requires authorization before being granted, access is modified when roles change, and access is removed promptly when a user leaves or no longer needs it.
controls.search_query: user provisioning deprovisioning offboarding access removal termination access review account lifecycle
controls.what_to_look_for: Evidence of a managed account lifecycle. Look for: provisioning process, access request approval, onboarding, offboarding, termination checklist, access revoked within 24 hours, periodic access review, recertification, deprovisioning automation.
controls.what_good_looks_like: CC6.2 requires registering and authorizing new internal and external users before granting access, and removing access when no longer required. CC6.3 requires modifying access based on roles. Best practice: approval-gated provisioning, same-day deprovisioning at termination, and quarterly access reviews.

scoring_guide.pass: Approval-based provisioning and prompt deprovisioning with review cadence described
scoring_guide.partial: Lifecycle process mentioned but timeliness or review frequency unclear
scoring_guide.fail: Evidence that departed users retain access or access is never reviewed
scoring_guide.no_evidence: No mention of account provisioning, offboarding, or access reviews

controls.title: Data protected in transit and at rest
controls.description: The vendor encrypts data moving over networks and data stored in its systems, and defends system boundaries against external threats.
controls.search_query: encryption TLS HTTPS data at rest AES encryption in transit firewall boundary protection
controls.what_to_look_for: Evidence of encryption and boundary defenses. Look for: TLS 1.2, TLS 1.3, HTTPS, AES-256, encryption at rest, encrypted backups, key management, KMS, firewall, WAF, VPN, network segmentation, intrusion prevention.
controls.what_good_looks_like: CC6.6 requires implementing boundary protection against threats from outside system boundaries. CC6.7 requires restricting the transmission and movement of information to authorized users and processes, protecting it during transmission. Best practice: TLS 1.2+ everywhere, AES-256 at rest including backups, and managed encryption keys separate from data.

scoring_guide.pass: Encryption in transit and at rest both confirmed with algorithms or versions
scoring_guide.partial: Encryption mentioned but only one of transit/rest covered, or details vague
scoring_guide.fail: Evidence of unencrypted storage or transmission
scoring_guide.no_evidence: No mention of encryption or boundary protection

controls.title: Vulnerabilities and anomalies monitored
controls.description: The vendor monitors infrastructure for vulnerabilities, configuration changes, and anomalous activity that could indicate a security event, using detection tooling and defined alerting.
controls.search_query: vulnerability scanning security monitoring anomaly detection intrusion detection SIEM alerting penetration testing
controls.what_to_look_for: Evidence of proactive detection. Look for: vulnerability scanning, penetration testing, SIEM, intrusion detection, IDS, security monitoring, anomaly detection, log analysis, alerting rules, security operations center, threat intelligence.
controls.what_good_looks_like: CC7.1 requires detection and monitoring procedures to identify changes and susceptibilities to new vulnerabilities. CC7.2 requires monitoring for anomalies indicative of malicious acts or errors. Best practice: at least monthly automated scanning, annual independent penetration tests, centralized log monitoring with automated alerts, and 24/7 escalation.

scoring_guide.pass: Scanning and continuous monitoring described with tooling and cadence
scoring_guide.partial: Monitoring or scanning mentioned but coverage, cadence, or alerting unclear
scoring_guide.fail: Evidence of no proactive vulnerability or anomaly detection
scoring_guide.no_evidence: No mention of monitoring, scanning, or detection

controls.title: Security incidents responded to and remediated
controls.description: The vendor evaluates security events, has a documented incident response program to contain and remediate incidents, communicates with affected parties, and recovers operations.
controls.search_query: incident response plan security incident breach notification containment remediation post-incident review
controls.what_to_look_for: Evidence of a working incident response capability. Look for: incident response plan, incident response team, severity classification, containment, eradication, recovery, breach notification, customer notification timelines, post-mortem, lessons learned, tabletop exercises.
controls.what_good_looks_like: CC7.3 requires evaluating security events to determine whether they are incidents. CC7.4 requires responding through a defined incident response program. CC7.5 requires identifying, developing, and implementing recovery activities. Best practice: documented and annually tested IR plan, defined roles, customer notification commitments (e.g. 72 hours), and post-incident reviews feeding improvements.

scoring_guide.pass: Documented IR program with roles, notification timelines, and testing
scoring_guide.partial: IR capability mentioned but testing, timelines, or communication unclear
scoring_guide.fail: Evidence of ad-hoc or absent incident handling
scoring_guide.no_evidence: No mention of incident response or breach handling

controls.title: Backup, recovery, and availability tested
controls.description: The vendor backs up data, maintains recovery infrastructure and plans (including environmental protections), and periodically tests that recovery actually works.
controls.search_query: backup disaster recovery business continuity RTO RPO recovery testing failover redundancy
controls.what_to_look_for: Evidence of availability and recoverability. Look for: backup frequency, encrypted backups, disaster recovery plan, business continuity plan, RTO, RPO, failover, multi-region, redundancy, recovery testing, DR exercises, uptime SLA.
controls.what_good_looks_like: A1.2 requires environmental protections, software, data backup processes, and recovery infrastructure. A1.3 requires testing recovery plan procedures. Best practice: automated daily backups stored separately, defined RTO/RPO, at least annual DR tests with documented results, and a published availability SLA.

scoring_guide.pass: Backups, DR plan, and recovery testing all described with targets
scoring_guide.partial: Backups or DR mentioned but testing or recovery targets unclear
scoring_guide.fail: Evidence that backups or recovery capability do not exist
scoring_guide.no_evidence: No mention of backups, disaster recovery, or continuity

controls.title: Change management process enforced
controls.description: Changes to infrastructure, software, and configurations are authorized, tested, and approved before deployment, with the ability to roll back and with production access separated from development.
controls.search_query: change management change approval code review testing deployment pipeline rollback separation of environments
controls.what_to_look_for: Evidence of controlled change. Look for: change management policy, change approval, CAB, peer code review, pull request review, CI/CD pipeline, automated testing before deploy, staging environment, rollback procedures, segregation of duties between dev and production, emergency change process.
controls.what_good_looks_like: CC8.1 requires authorizing, designing, developing, testing, approving, and implementing changes to infrastructure, data, and software. Best practice: mandatory peer review, automated test gates in CI/CD, separate staging and production environments, documented rollback, and restricted production deploy rights.

scoring_guide.pass: Change process described with review, testing, and approval gates
scoring_guide.partial: Change control mentioned but gates, environments, or approvals unclear
scoring_guide.fail: Evidence of unreviewed or direct-to-production changes
scoring_guide.no_evidence: No mention of change management or deployment controls

controls.title: Subprocessor and vendor risk managed
controls.description: The vendor assesses and manages the risks of its own vendors and subprocessors: due diligence before onboarding, contractual security commitments, and ongoing monitoring.
controls.search_query: subprocessor vendor management third-party risk due diligence supplier assessment vendor security review
controls.what_to_look_for: Evidence of third-party risk management. Look for: subprocessor list, vendor due diligence, third-party risk assessment, vendor security questionnaires, data processing agreements, DPA, contractual security requirements, annual vendor reviews, subprocessor change notification.
controls.what_good_looks_like: CC9.2 requires assessing and managing risks associated with vendors and business partners. Best practice: security due diligence before engaging vendors, DPAs with all subprocessors, a published subprocessor list with change notifications, and periodic reassessment of critical vendors.

scoring_guide.pass: Vendor risk program described with due diligence, contracts, and ongoing review
scoring_guide.partial: Vendor management mentioned but diligence depth or recurrence unclear
scoring_guide.fail: Evidence that third parties are engaged without security review
scoring_guide.no_evidence: No mention of vendor or subprocessor risk management
